genetic_lineage.overview: Petit Verdot is an ancient Bordeaux variety with no confirmed parentage. DNA studies suggest it may be a parent of several other Bordeaux varieties.

parents.name: Unknown
parents.notes: No parentage established. Indigenous to the Bordeaux region.

offspring.name: Gros Verdot
offspring.notes: Related variety, possibly offspring or sharing a parent.

genetic_lineage.genetic_significance: Petit Verdot's ancient origins and role in Bordeaux blends make it historically significant. The variety's late ripening limited its use in Bordeaux but made it valuable in warmer New World regions.

clones.name: Clone 400
clones.characteristics: Standard French clone with good color extraction and tannic structure.

clones.name: Clone 401
clones.characteristics: Selected for aromatic intensity, particularly violet notes.

clones.name: Clone 1
clones.characteristics: California selection adapted to warmer conditions. Reliable ripening.

clones.name: Médoc Heritage Selection
clones.characteristics: Traditional massal selections. Maximum aromatic complexity.

terroir.effect: Classic Médoc terroir. Gravel provides drainage and heat retention for this late-ripening variety.
Found in warmer New World sites. Produces powerful, concentrated wines.
Emerging terroir in regions like Spain. Adds mineral complexity.

susceptibilities.notes: Very susceptible to poor fruit set in cool, wet conditions.
susceptibilities.impact_on_wine: Major factor limiting yields and Bordeaux plantings

resistances.notes: Late ripening makes it well-suited to warm climates.
Deep roots help tolerate dry conditions.

disease_profile.general_notes: Petit Verdot is the latest-ripening of the Bordeaux varieties, rarely achieving full ripeness in cooler Bordeaux vintages. The variety provides intense color, firm tannins, and distinctive violet aromatics to blends. In warm climates like California, Australia, and Spain, it can be successfully vinified as a varietal.

regional_expressions.style_summary: Traditional home of Petit Verdot, used in small percentages (typically 1-5%) in Bordeaux blends to add color, structure, and violet aromatics. Rarely ripens fully, limiting its role. Climate change may increase its importance.
notable_producers: Most Grand Cru classé estates (as component)

regional_expressions.style_summary: California's warm climate allows Petit Verdot to fully ripen, enabling varietal bottlings. Wines are powerful, deeply colored, with pronounced violet and dark fruit character. Increasingly popular as producers recognize its potential.
Australian producers increasingly vinify Petit Verdot as a varietal, taking advantage of warm conditions. Powerful, deeply colored wines with New World generosity.
Spanish producers are exploring Petit Verdot both as a blending grape and varietal. The warm climate ensures ripeness, and innovative winemakers are showcasing its potential.
